critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design. I will work through each potential issue systematically, grouping by root cause.

---

**Group 1: Test Set Contamination via Confirmatory Peek**

The design describes a 'confirmatory pass' on the test set (months 31–36) before final retraining. The stated purpose is to verify the selected validation configuration 'maintains its advantage under the most recent routing distribution.' This is a meaningful concern — validation period drift is real — but the mechanism introduced to address it creates a different problem.

The specific claim: using the test set once for confirmation, without further search, does not inflate reported performance.

Why this might be wrong: This is a one-shot comparison of two models on the held-out test window. Even a single look creates an implicit selection event: a configuration is *confirmed* only if it performs acceptably on the test set. If it does not — even without changing hyperparameters — one would presumably not proceed with that configuration without revision, which would require another test-set evaluation. The word 'confirmatory' disguises a decision node. The design does not specify what happens when confirmation fails (i.e., when the validation-period winner underperforms on months 31–36), and that ambiguity is where contamination lives. This is structurally different from a clean single pass.

Evidence one way or the other: The design should pre-specify what 'confirmation failure' means and what action is taken. If the answer is 'we proceed regardless,' the confirmation step is informationally void and can be removed. If the answer is 'we revise,' then the test set is participating in model selection and reported test metrics are optimistic. The protocol must commit to one of these before evaluation begins.

---

**Group 2: Label Freeze Creates a Systematic Asymmetry Between Models**

The design freezes the label set at month 18 to prevent label drift. The training set covers months 1–24.

The specific claim: freezing the label set at month 18 prevents label drift without distorting the training signal.

Why this might be wrong: Tickets from months 19–24 that were resolved by teams created or renamed after month 18 are either dropped, relabeled to a predecessor queue, or labeled 'other.' The design does not specify which. If they are dropped, the training set has a systematic gap in the most recent six months of training data — precisely the data most predictive of test-period behavior. If they are relabeled, the labels are noisy in a temporally structured way: the noisiest labels are in the most recent training period. This matters asymmetrically: TF-IDF is shallower and may be less sensitive to noisy labels in recent months, while the transformer's fine-tuning on the full sequence may overfit to the label noise pattern in months 19–24. The freeze point is independent of both the train/val split and the team reorganization timeline, so its interaction with the data is uncharacterized.

Evidence one way or the other: Report the volume of tickets affected by the month-18 label freeze in each split window. A large affected volume in months 19–24 relative to months 1–18 would suggest the asymmetry is material. Separately, sensitivity analysis on the freeze point (e.g., freeze at month 12 vs. month 18 vs. month 24) would bound the effect.

---

**Group 3: Reply History as a Source of Structural Leakage**

Both models include agent reply history as part of the concatenated input text.

The specific claim: including reply history is a legitimate feature because it is available at routing time.

Why this might be wrong: For initial routing, reply history is empty — the ticket has just arrived. For rerouting events (which are excluded from labels), reply history is partially populated. The design excludes reassigned tickets, but resolved tickets that were answered before being closed may have reply history from the resolving team. Crucially, agent reply text often contains team-identifying signals: internal routing codes, queue-specific greetings, team signatures, or product-area jargon specific to the resolving team. A transformer fine-tuned on the full text learns to map these team-identifying linguistic artifacts in the reply history to the label — which is the same team. This is not temporal leakage in the train/test sense, but it is structural leakage: the model learns 'team X's agents write like X' rather than 'this problem type belongs to team X.' The baseline (TF-IDF) also has this signal, but the transformer's sequence modeling is better positioned to exploit it.

This directly undermines the claim that transformer advantage reflects 'genuine semantic understanding.' At test time in production, a new arriving ticket has no reply history, so any advantage attributable to reply-history leakage will not replicate.

Evidence one way or the other: Ablate reply history. Train both models on subject + body only, and on subject + body + reply history, and compare the delta in accuracy. A large performance drop when reply history is removed — larger for the transformer than the baseline — is diagnostic of this failure mode. The confound control section mentions ticket length but does not mention this ablation, which is the more direct test.

---

**Group 4: Weighted F1 as Primary Metric Suppresses the Hypothesis Under Test**

The primary metric is weighted F1. The justification is that it reflects the actual ticket mix.

The specific claim: weighted F1 is the appropriate primary metric because it reflects production routing quality.

Why this might be wrong: If 30–40% of tickets go to one or two queues and the transformer achieves the same accuracy as the baseline on those dominant queues but substantially outperforms on minority queues, weighted F1 will understate the transformer advantage. More importantly, the hypothesis includes the claim that the transformer enables 'faster first-response resolution.' This claim is most plausible for hard-to-route tickets — ambiguous language, new product areas, edge cases — which are disproportionately concentrated in minority queues. Weighted F1 is designed to be insensitive to exactly this population. The design includes macro-F1 as secondary, but secondary metrics do not govern model selection (which is done by weighted F1 on the validation set). A model that sacrifices minority-queue performance to improve majority-queue performance will be selected preferentially, which may not align with the business case for the transformer.

This is not a fatal flaw if the business priority is genuinely the high-volume queues. But the design simultaneously claims the transformer is better for resolution time — a claim that requires it to be better on hard cases — while selecting models on a metric that discounts hard cases. The two claims are in tension.

Evidence one way or the other: Report the volume and FRT improvement breakdown by queue tier (top 3 queues by volume vs. the rest). If FRT improvement is concentrated in minority queues but the transformer is selected and justified on weighted F1, the operational claim is unsupported by the selection criterion.

---

**Summary Assessment:**

Issue 3 (reply history leakage) is the most structurally damaging because it directly undermines the core claim that transformer advantage reflects semantic understanding of the routing problem rather than exploitation of a signal that is absent at deployment time. Issue 1 (confirmatory peek) is a genuine protocol flaw that requires pre-specification to be defensible. Issues 2 and 4 are real but more bounded — they affect interpretation and magnitude rather than the validity of the comparison. Overall, the design is sophisticated but has at least one genuine validity threat (reply history) and one protocol ambiguity (confirmation step) that need to be addressed before the results can be trusted.
all_issues_raised: The 'confirmatory pass' on the test set introduces an implicit selection decision point whose failure branch is unspecified, creating potential test-set contamination.
The label freeze at month 18 may introduce temporally structured label noise in months 19–24 of training data, with an asymmetric effect on the transformer vs. the baseline.

Agent reply history in the input text likely contains team-identifying linguistic artifacts that constitute structural leakage: the model may learn team signatures rather than routing semantics, and this signal is absent on new arriving tickets in production.
Selecting models by weighted F1 is in tension with the FRT improvement claim, which is most plausible for minority queues that weighted F1 systematically discounts.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 'confirmatory pass' on the test set introduces an implicit selection decision point whose failure branch is unspecified, creating potential test-set contamination.
The label freeze at month 18 may introduce temporally structured label noise in months 19–24 of training data, with an asymmetric effect on the transformer vs. the baseline.

Agent reply history in the input text likely contains team-identifying linguistic artifacts that constitute structural leakage: the model may learn team signatures rather than routing semantics, and this signal is absent on new arriving tickets in production.
Selecting models by weighted F1 is in tension with the FRT improvement claim, which is most plausible for minority queues that weighted F1 systematically discounts.
